([/ˈfaɪ, ˈfiː/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q1860_(eng)-Flame,_not_lame-Phi.wav "File:LL-Q1860 (eng)-Flame, not lame-Phi.wav") [*FY, FEE*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key");[[1]](#cite_note-1) uppercase **Φ**, lowercase **φ** or **ϕ**; [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): ϕεῖ *pheî* [[pʰéî̯]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Greek "Help:IPA/Greek"); [Modern Greek](/wiki/Modern_Greek "Modern Greek"): φι *fi* [[fi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Greek "Help:IPA/Greek")) is the twenty-first letter of the [Greek alphabet](/wiki/Greek_alphabet "Greek alphabet").

[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Phi%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Phi%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Phi%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Phi%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(August 2020)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Twenty-first letter in the Greek alphabet

Archaic form of Phi

**Phi** ([/ˈfaɪ, ˈfiː/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q1860_(eng)-Flame,_not_lame-Phi.wav "File:LL-Q1860 (eng)-Flame, not lame-Phi.wav") [*FY, FEE*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key");[[1]](#cite_note-1) uppercase **Φ**, lowercase **φ** or **ϕ**; [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): ϕεῖ *pheî* [[pʰéî̯]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Greek "Help:IPA/Greek"); [Modern Greek](/wiki/Modern_Greek "Modern Greek"): φι *fi* [[fi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Greek "Help:IPA/Greek")) is the twenty-first letter of the [Greek alphabet](/wiki/Greek_alphabet "Greek alphabet").

In [Archaic](/wiki/Archaic_Greek "Archaic Greek") and [Classical Greek](/wiki/Classical_Greek "Classical Greek") (c. 9th to 4th century BC), it represented an [aspirated](/wiki/Aspiration_(phonetics) "Aspiration (phonetics)") [voiceless bilabial plosive](/wiki/Voiceless_bilabial_plosive "Voiceless bilabial plosive") ([pʰ]), which was the origin of its [usual romanization](/wiki/Romanization_of_Greek#Ancient_Greek "Romanization of Greek") as ⟨ph⟩. During the later part of [Classical Antiquity](/wiki/Classical_Antiquity "Classical Antiquity"), in [Koine Greek](/wiki/Koine_Greek "Koine Greek") (c. 4th century BC to 4th century AD), its pronunciation shifted to a [voiceless bilabial fricative](/wiki/Voiceless_bilabial_fricative "Voiceless bilabial fricative") ([ɸ]), and by the [Byzantine Greek](/wiki/Byzantine_Greek "Byzantine Greek") period (c. 4th century AD to 15th century AD) it developed its modern pronunciation as a [voiceless labiodental fricative](/wiki/Voiceless_labiodental_fricative "Voiceless labiodental fricative") ([f]).
The [romanization](/wiki/Romanization_of_Greek#Modern_Greek "Romanization of Greek") of the Modern Greek phoneme is therefore usually ⟨f⟩.

It may be that phi originated as the letter [qoppa](/wiki/Qoppa "Qoppa") (Ϙ, ϙ), and initially represented the sound /kʷʰ/ before shifting to Classical Greek [[pʰ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Greek "Help:IPA/Greek").[[2]](#cite_note-2) In traditional [Greek numerals](/wiki/Greek_numerals "Greek numerals"), phi has a value of 500 (φʹ) or 500,000 (͵φ). The Cyrillic letter [Ef](/wiki/Ef_(Cyrillic) "Ef (Cyrillic)") (Ф, ф) descends from phi.

Like other Greek letters, lowercase phi (encoded as the [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode") character U+03C6 φ GREEK SMALL LETTER PHI) is used as a mathematical or scientific symbol. [Some uses](#only_the_stroked_glyph_is_considered_correct_in_this_use) require the old-fashioned 'closed' glyph, which is separately encoded as the Unicode character U+03D5 ϕ GREEK PHI SYMBOL.

## Use as a symbol

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Phi "Special:EditPage/Phi") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Phi"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Phi%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Phi%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Phi%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Phi%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Phi%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Phi%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(August 2020)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

### In lowercase

The lowercase letter φ (or its variant, ϕ or ɸ) is often used to represent the following:

* The letter phi is commonly used in [physics](/wiki/Physics "Physics") to represent [wave functions](/wiki/Wave_function "Wave function") in [quantum mechanics](/wiki/Quantum_mechanics "Quantum mechanics"), such as in the [Schrödinger equation](/wiki/Schr%C3%B6dinger_equation "Schrödinger equation") and [bra–ket notation](/wiki/Bra%E2%80%93ket_notation "Bra–ket notation"): ![{\displaystyle \langle \phi |\psi \rangle }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7d9869dca1c0dd01660954d2214ed16ca6115faf).
* The [golden ratio](/wiki/Golden_ratio "Golden ratio") ![{\displaystyle {\tfrac {1+{\sqrt {5}}}{2}}\approx }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8b01489b13a55e36d1279cc65711a181c08fd535) 1.618033988749894848204586834... in mathematics,[[3]](#cite_note-3) art, and architecture. (Its reciprocal, 1/φ, is ![{\displaystyle {\tfrac {{\sqrt {5}}-1}{2}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ee010c2d7516c770044bc4d31af29f356fa8be0f) and is equal to φ − 1.)
* [Euler's totient function](/wiki/Euler%27s_totient_function "Euler's totient function") *φ*(*n*) in number theory;[[4]](#cite_note-4) also called *Euler's phi function*.
* The [cyclotomic polynomial](/wiki/Cyclotomic_polynomial "Cyclotomic polynomial") functions Φ*n*(*x*) of algebra.
* The number of [electrical phases](/wiki/Electrical_phase "Electrical phase") in a power system in [electrical engineering](/wiki/Electrical_engineering "Electrical engineering"), for example 1ϕ for [single phase](/wiki/Single-phase_electric_power "Single-phase electric power"), 3ϕ for [three phase](/wiki/Three-phase_electric_power "Three-phase electric power").
* In algebra, [group](/wiki/Group_(mathematics) "Group (mathematics)") or [ring](/wiki/Ring_(mathematics) "Ring (mathematics)") [homomorphisms](/wiki/Homomorphism "Homomorphism")
* In [probability theory](/wiki/Probability_theory "Probability theory"), ![{\textstyle \varphi (x)=(2\pi )^{-{\frac {1}{2}}}e^{-{\frac {x^{2}}{2}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/138ba617cb22f113e416d6f801fa12e8e1509334) is the [probability density function](/wiki/Probability_density_function "Probability density function") of the [standard normal distribution](/wiki/Standard_normal_distribution "Standard normal distribution").
* In [probability theory](/wiki/Probability_theory "Probability theory"), *φ**X*(*t*) = E[*e**itX*] is the [characteristic function](/wiki/Characteristic_function_(probability_theory) "Characteristic function (probability theory)") of a random variable *X*.
* An [angle](/wiki/Angle "Angle"), typically the second angle mentioned, after *θ* ([theta](/wiki/Theta "Theta")).[[5]](#cite_note-5) It can denote:
  + The argument of a [complex number](/wiki/Complex_number "Complex number").
  + The [phase of a wave](/wiki/Phase_(waves) "Phase (waves)") in signal processing.
  + In [spherical coordinates](/wiki/Spherical_coordinates "Spherical coordinates"), mathematicians usually refer to ϕ as the polar angle (from the *z*-[axis](/wiki/Coordinate_axis "Coordinate axis")). The convention in physics is to use ϕ as the azimuthal angle (from the *x*-axis).
  + One of the [dihedral angles](/wiki/Dihedral_angle "Dihedral angle") in the backbones of [proteins](/wiki/Protein "Protein") in a [Ramachandran plot](/wiki/Ramachandran_plot "Ramachandran plot")
  + Internal or effective [angle of friction](/wiki/Angle_of_friction "Angle of friction").
  + In [cartography](/wiki/Cartography "Cartography") and [navigation](/wiki/Navigation "Navigation"), ɸ denotes [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"); likewise, in [geodesy](/wiki/Geodesy "Geodesy") it denotes [geodetic latitude](/wiki/Geodetic_latitude "Geodetic latitude").
  + In [ship stability](/wiki/Ship_stability "Ship stability"), ɸ represents the [angle of list](/wiki/Angle_of_list "Angle of list").
  + In [aircraft flight mechanics](/wiki/Aircraft_flight_mechanics "Aircraft flight mechanics") as the symbol for [bank angle](/wiki/Bank_angle "Bank angle") (sometimes represented with the letter theta, which is also used for pitch angle).
* The [work function](/wiki/Work_function "Work function") of a surface, in [solid-state physics](/wiki/Solid-state_physics "Solid-state physics").
* A [shorthand](/wiki/Shorthand "Shorthand") representation for an [aromatic](/wiki/Aromatic "Aromatic") [functional group](/wiki/Functional_group "Functional group") in [organic chemistry](/wiki/Organic_chemistry "Organic chemistry").
* The [fugacity](/wiki/Fugacity "Fugacity") coefficient in [thermodynamics](/wiki/Thermodynamics "Thermodynamics").
* The ratio of [free energy](/wiki/Thermodynamic_free_energy "Thermodynamic free energy") destabilizations of protein [mutants](/wiki/Mutant "Mutant") in [phi value analysis](/wiki/Phi_value_analysis "Phi value analysis").
* In combustion engineering, [fuel–air equivalence ratio](/wiki/Air%E2%80%93fuel_ratio#Fuel–air_equivalence_ratio_(ϕ) "Air–fuel ratio"). The ratio between the actual fuel-air ratio to the stoichiometric fuel-air ratio.
* In granulometry, [sedimentology](/wiki/Sedimentology "Sedimentology"), and [soil engineering](/wiki/Soil_engineering "Soil engineering"), φ is a logarithmic unit of sediment [grain size](/wiki/Grain_size "Grain size"), defined such that a change of 1 φ in grain size corresponds to a factor of 2 in grain diameter.
* A sentence in [first-order logic](/wiki/First-order_logic "First-order logic").
* The [Veblen function](/wiki/Veblen_function "Veblen function") in [set theory](/wiki/Set_theory "Set theory").
* [Porosity](/wiki/Porosity "Porosity") in geology and [hydrology](/wiki/Hydrology "Hydrology").
* Strength (or resistance) reduction factor in [structural engineering](/wiki/Structural_engineering "Structural engineering"), used to account for statistical variabilities in materials and construction methods.
* The symbol for a [voiceless bilabial fricative](/wiki/Voiceless_bilabial_fricative "Voiceless bilabial fricative") in the [International Phonetic Alphabet](/wiki/International_Phonetic_Alphabet "International Phonetic Alphabet") (using the form ɸ).
* In [philosophy](/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy"), φ is often used as shorthand for a [generic act](/w/index.php?title=Generic_act&action=edit&redlink=1 "Generic act (page does not exist)"). (Also in uppercase.)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* In [perceptual psychology](/wiki/Perceptual_psychology "Perceptual psychology"), the [phi phenomenon](/wiki/Phi_phenomenon "Phi phenomenon") is the apparent motion caused by the successive viewing of stationary objects, such as the frames of a motion picture.
* In linguistics, [φ-features](/wiki/Phi_features "Phi features") denote features such as case, number and gender in which adjectives and pronouns agree with nouns.
* In [lexical-functional grammar](/wiki/Lexical-functional_grammar "Lexical-functional grammar"), the function that maps elements from the [c-structure](/w/index.php?title=C-structure&action=edit&redlink=1 "C-structure (page does not exist)") to the [f-structure](/w/index.php?title=F-structure&action=edit&redlink=1 "F-structure (page does not exist)").
* In [ecology](/wiki/Ecology "Ecology"), [site survival probability](/w/index.php?title=Site_survival_probability&action=edit&redlink=1 "Site survival probability (page does not exist)"), or the probability that a species will continue to occupy a site if it was there the previous year.
* The logo of [La France Insoumise](/wiki/La_France_Insoumise "La France Insoumise"), a leftist French [political party](/wiki/Political_party "Political party").
* An abbreviation for the word [bacteriophage](/wiki/Bacteriophage "Bacteriophage")
  + Mϕ is used as an abbreviation for the word [macrophage](/wiki/Macrophage "Macrophage")

### In uppercase

The uppercase Φ is used as a symbol for:

* The [cumulative distribution function](/wiki/Cumulative_distribution_function "Cumulative distribution function") (cdf) of [standard normal distribution](/wiki/Standard_normal_distribution "Standard normal distribution") in statistics.
* The [magnetic flux](/wiki/Magnetic_flux "Magnetic flux") and [electric flux](/wiki/Electric_flux "Electric flux") in physics, with subscripts distinguishing the two.
* [Quantum yield](/wiki/Quantum_yield "Quantum yield"), the number of times a specific [event](/wiki/Event_(particle_physics) "Event (particle physics)") occurs per [photon](/wiki/Photon "Photon") absorbed by a [system](/wiki/Quantum_system "Quantum system").[[6]](#cite_note-6)
* In [philosophy](/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy"), Φ is often used as shorthand for a [generic act](/w/index.php?title=Generic_act&action=edit&redlink=1 "Generic act (page does not exist)"). (Also in lowercase.)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* A common symbol for the parametrization of a surface in vector [calculus](/wiki/Calculus "Calculus").
* In [Lacanian algebra](/wiki/Lacanian_algebra "Lacanian algebra"), Φ stands for the imaginary phallus and also represents phallic signification; −Φ stands in for castration.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Phi#Dubious "Talk:Phi")*]
* The [diameter symbol](/wiki/Diameter_symbol "Diameter symbol") in engineering, [⌀](/wiki/%E2%8C%80 "⌀"), is often erroneously referred to as "phi", and the diameter symbol is sometimes erroneously typeset as Φ. This symbol is used to indicate the diameter of a circular section; for example, "⌀14" means the diameter of the [circle](/wiki/Circle "Circle") is 14 [units](/wiki/Units_of_measurement "Units of measurement").
* The [null sign](/wiki/Null_sign "Null sign"), ∅, which denotes the empty set in mathematics is sometimes also erroneously mistaken for phi.
* A [clock signal](/wiki/Clock_signal "Clock signal") in electronics is often called Phi or uses the symbol.[[8]](#cite_note-8)
* In emulsion and suspension science, Φ often denotes the volume fraction of the dispersed phase.[[9]](#cite_note-9)
* Archaically used in chemistry for [phenyl group](/wiki/Phenyl_group "Phenyl group").
* [Integrated information theory](/wiki/Integrated_information_theory "Integrated information theory") (IIT), a mathematical model for consciousness

## Unicode

In [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode"), there are multiple forms of the phi letter:

| Character | Name | Correct appearance | Your browser | [LaTeX](/wiki/LaTeX "LaTeX") | Usage |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `U+03A6` | GREEK CAPITAL LETTER PHI | {\displaystyle \Phi \,\!} | Φ | {\displaystyle \Phi } | Used in Greek texts |
| `U+03C6` | GREEK SMALL LETTER PHI | or | φ | {\displaystyle \varphi } or {\displaystyle \phi } | Used in Greek texts |
| `U+03D5` | GREEK PHI SYMBOL | ϕ | ϕ (*ϕ*) | {\displaystyle \phi } | Intended for use in modern (monotonic) Greek texts. Used italicized in mathematical and technical contexts when the "straight-line" variant glyph is preferred.[[10]](#cite_note-unicode-math-10) |
| `U+0278` | LATIN SMALL LETTER PHI |  | ɸ | {\displaystyle } | Used in [IPA](/wiki/International_Phonetic_Alphabet "International Phonetic Alphabet") to denote a [voiceless bilabial fricative](/wiki/Voiceless_bilabial_fricative "Voiceless bilabial fricative") |

In ordinary Greek text, the character U+03C6 φ is used exclusively, though this character has considerable glyphic variation, sometimes represented with a glyph more like the representative glyph shown for U+03C6 (φ, the "loopy" or "open" form), and less often with a glyph more like the representative glyph shown for U+03D5 (ϕ, the "stroked" or "closed" form).

Because Unicode represents a character in an abstract way, the choice between glyphs is purely a matter of font design. While some Greek typefaces, most notably those in the [Porson](/wiki/Porson_(typeface) "Porson (typeface)") family (used widely in editions of classical Greek texts), have a "stroked" glyph in this position ([![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Greek_phi_Porson.svg/20px-Greek_phi_Porson.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Greek_phi_Porson.svg)), most other typefaces have "loopy" glyphs. This also applies to the "Didot" (or "*apla*") typefaces employed in most Greek book printing ([![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Greek_phi_Didot.svg/20px-Greek_phi_Didot.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Greek_phi_Didot.svg)), as well as the "Neohellenic" typeface often used for ancient texts ([![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Greek_phi_Neohellenic.svg/20px-Greek_phi_Neohellenic.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Greek_phi_Neohellenic.svg)).

It is necessary to have the stroked glyph available for some mathematical uses, and U+03D5 GREEK PHI SYMBOL is designed for this function. Prior to Unicode version 3.0 (1998), the glyph assignments in the Unicode code charts were the reverse, and thus older fonts may still show a loopy form ![{\displaystyle \varphi }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/33ee699558d09cf9d653f6351f9fda0b2f4aaa3e) at U+03D5.[[10]](#cite_note-unicode-math-10)

For use as a phonetic symbol in [IPA](/wiki/International_Phonetic_Alphabet "International Phonetic Alphabet"), Unicode has a separate code point U+0278, LATIN SMALL LETTER PHI, because only the stroked glyph is considered correct in this use. It typically appears in a form adapted to a Latin typographic environment, with a more upright shape than normal Greek letters and with [serifs](/wiki/Serif "Serif") at the top and bottom.

In [LaTeX](/wiki/LaTeX "LaTeX"), the math symbols are `\Phi` (![{\displaystyle \Phi \,\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/21242b7aa20bff076c14a47d1a2d9f0afb35f165)), `\phi` (![{\displaystyle \phi \,\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/30ae608cf0ade0577802bc4ffa026a29ab35c365)), and `\varphi` (![{\displaystyle \varphi \,\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ca38cae992cf33a25f380c941c0e3f34193704ad)).

The Unicode standard includes the following variants of phi and phi-like characters:

* U+0278 ɸ LATIN SMALL LETTER PHI
* U+03A6 Φ GREEK CAPITAL LETTER PHI (Φ)
* U+03C6 φ GREEK SMALL LETTER PHI (φ)
* U+03D5 ϕ GREEK PHI SYMBOL (ϕ, ϕ, ϕ)
* U+1D60 ᵠ MODIFIER LETTER SMALL GREEK PHI
* U+1D69 ᵩ GREEK SUBSCRIPT SMALL LETTER PHI
* U+1DB2 ᶲ MODIFIER LETTER SMALL PHI
* U+2C77 ⱷ LATIN SMALL LETTER TAILLESS PHI
* U+2CAA Ⲫ COPTIC CAPITAL LETTER FI
* U+2CAB ⲫ COPTIC SMALL LETTER FI
* U+1D6BD 𝚽 MATHEMATICAL BOLD CAPITAL PHI
* U+1D6D7 𝛗 MATHEMATICAL BOLD SMALL PHI
* U+1D6DF 𝛟 MATHEMATICAL BOLD PHI SYMBOL
* U+1D6F7 𝛷 MATHEMATICAL ITALIC CAPITAL PHI
* U+1D711 𝜑 MATHEMATICAL ITALIC SMALL PHI
* U+1D719 𝜙 MATHEMATICAL ITALIC PHI SYMBOL
* U+1D731 𝜱 MATHEMATICAL BOLD ITALIC CAPITAL PHI
* U+1D74B 𝝋 MATHEMATICAL BOLD ITALIC SMALL PHI
* U+1D753 𝝓 MATHEMATICAL BOLD ITALIC PHI SYMBOL
* U+1D76B 𝝫 MATHEMATICAL SANS-SERIF BOLD CAPITAL PHI
* U+1D785 𝞅 MATHEMATICAL SANS-SERIF BOLD SMALL PHI
* U+1D78D 𝞍 MATHEMATICAL SANS-SERIF BOLD PHI SYMBOL
* U+1D7A5 𝞥 MATHEMATICAL SANS-SERIF BOLD ITALIC CAPITAL PHI
* U+1D7BF 𝞿 MATHEMATICAL SANS-SERIF BOLD ITALIC SMALL PHI
* U+1D7C7 𝟇 MATHEMATICAL SANS-SERIF BOLD ITALIC PHI SYMBOL
